topic: Embedded - no_std with the alloc Crate

turns.content: Hey, I’m working on a small embedded device where I want to use Rust but need it to be as lightweight as possible. So far, I’ve been thinking of using #![no_std] and the alloc crate for heap allocation. But I’m unsure about how to tie everything together—how do I provide a global allocator and actually use Vec<String> or similar?

That’s a great start! When you’re in no_std mode, Rust doesn’t have the standard library by default. That means you’ll need to manually choose your heap allocator since the system won’t provide one for you. The alloc crate is perfect here; it provides an API similar to std::alloc but without depending on any other parts of the standard lib.
So, do I just add extern crate alloc in my Cargo.toml and then import Vec and String from there?

Yes! That’s one way to go. You can use `use alloc::vec::Vec; use alloc::string::String;`. But remember, you’ll also need to provide a heap allocator—otherwise, calling Vec or String will panic because there’s no default allocator.
I see. So I have to implement an allocator and register it? That sounds tricky.

Exactly! You’ll need to define a type that implements the `GlobalAlloc` trait from alloc::alloc, then register it via `#[global_allocator]`. It’s kind of like hooking into the runtime so that every Vec or String allocation uses your custom allocator.
Okay, but what if I’m targeting a specific embedded platform? Should I use something like embedded-alloc here?

Good question. `embedded-alloc` is designed specifically for constrained environments and can simplify things. It provides a drop-in heap allocator that you can easily integrate into your no_std build. You just include it, initialize it in your app startup, and forget about allocators—Rust will use the embedded one.
Ah, so I’d have to add `embedded-alloc` as a dependency and then init it early?

Yes! In your startup code (main.rs or lib.rs), you initialize the allocator. For example: `#[global_allocator] static ALLOCATOR: embedded_alloc::Heap = embedded_alloc::Heap::empty();` and then call `.init()` with a region of memory early on, before any Vecs or Strings get used.
Great. So I’ll have `embedded-alloc` manage the heap, and my code will be able to use Vec<String> without panicking? That’s it?

Exactly! Now you can write your embedded app with Rust vectors and strings, knowing they won’t consume much memory because the allocator is carefully tuned for constrained environments. And if performance becomes an issue later—say, you need lower latency—you’ll have a clear path to optimize or switch allocators without rewriting lots of code.

topic: Embedded - no_std with the alloc Crate

turns.content: Hey, I'm a game developer looking to port my project to Rust for better performance. It's a large C++ codebase, so I want it to be as fast and memory efficient as possible.

That sounds like an exciting move! Rust can indeed offer significant performance benefits over C++. One of the key challenges is making your project work in a constrained environment—like embedded devices without a standard library. Have you considered #![no_std]?
Yeah, I thought about that. But then again, my code uses std::Vec and String which won’t compile in no_std mode.

Exactly! That’s why you need to use the `alloc` crate with `#![no_std]`. It provides heap allocation support without pulling in the full standard library. You can then import Vec and String from there. Have you tried importing those?
I haven’t yet, but I’m curious—what’s the difference between `alloc` crate and `liballoc`? Also, do I need to use a heap allocator for embedded devices?

`liballoc` is part of the standard library, while `alloc` is an external crate that provides the core allocation abstractions. For embedded with limited memory and performance constraints, you’ll want to provide your own heap allocator via the `GlobalAlloc` trait instead of relying on a generic one. This gives you more control over fragmentation and safety.
Okay, so I’d write my own allocator that implements `GlobalAlloc`, right?

Yes! That’s one approach. Alternatively, you can use crates like `heapless` or `embedded-alloc` which provide fixed-size collections and heap allocators optimized for embedded systems. They’re designed to avoid dynamic memory allocation, reducing overhead.
Hmm, but wait—what if I still want to use Vec and String in my codebase? Do I need to replace all of them?

Nope! You can keep using `Vec<T>` and `String` from the `alloc` crate, but you must ensure your allocator is initialized before any allocation happens. Also, be aware that these types allocate on the heap, so they won’t work in all embedded contexts (like stack-only or memory-constrained environments).
Right, I see. So if my codebase relies heavily on dynamic allocation, should I stick with C++ or try to rewrite parts of it?

That depends! If the performance and memory benefits outweigh the complexity of rewriting everything in Rust, go for it. Otherwise, you might want to evaluate whether some modules can be ported incrementally while others remain in C++. Sometimes hybrid approaches work well.
Alright, let me try importing `alloc::vec::Vec` and `alloc::string::String` with my own allocator. If I get stuck, what’s the best way to debug allocation issues?

Good question! Start by initializing your allocator early in main or before any heap allocations occur. Use logging to track when and how many allocations happen. Also, consider using `heapless` types if you notice excessive fragmentation or memory usage.
Got it. What about error handling? Can I rely on Rust’s strong guarantees in no_std?

Absolutely! Rust enforces type safety and memory safety with its strict ownership rules, even without the standard library. Use `Result` extensively for error propagation—this is idiomatic in Rust regardless of whether you’re using `alloc` or not.
Thanks! That clears things up. Just one last question: should I consider using `#![no_std]` from the start, or wait until I’ve isolated the performance-critical parts?

Good point! If you’re already heavily invested in C++, you might want to port gradually. Start with small modules and replace them one by one as you identify bottlenecks. This way, you can test your Rust code without risking the entire project's stability.
